title: Realm Trade
description: One terse gazetteer line on what a realm sells, buys, and worries about — for rollable realm entries.
entries: Exports wool, worked leather, and salted mutton; imports iron, glass, and dye. The clip auction sets the year's mood.
Exports grain in barges beyond counting; imports timber and mercenaries. A bad harvest here is a famine two realms away.
Exports silver from three great mines; imports nearly everything else, at prices that follow the assay reports.
Exports timber, pitch, and masts; imports grain and wine. The tall-tree stands are a state secret and badly kept.
Exports wine and brandy under a jealously guarded seal; imports oak, cork, and bottle-glass. Counterfeiting the seal is piracy in law.
Exports salt from coastal pans; imports charcoal and cloth. The salt levy funds the fleet and everyone knows it.
Exports horses of a famous line; imports saddlery, grain, and veterinary scholars. Export of breeding stock is a capital matter.
Exports dried fish and whale oil; imports grain, hemp, and luck. The catch is tithed to the lighthouse keepers.
Exports copper and tin together — convenient for everyone's bronze; imports wool and wheat. The mine towns pay no other tax.
Exports fine cloth from a thousand looms; imports raw fiber, alum, and indigo. The pattern-books are guarded like heirs.
Exports cheese, butter, and hides; imports salt and ironmongery. The great wheels age in border caves claimed by both sides.
Exports paper, ink, and bound books; imports rag, gall, and gossip. The censor's office is the largest single customer.
Exports amber and furs from the cold interior; imports silver and grain. The winter road is open eight weeks and lawless for six.
Exports quarried marble and cut stone; imports everything that can't be made of stone, which its poets insist is little.
Exports olives, oil, and soap; imports timber and wheat. Groves are deeded by the individual tree.
Exports spice from terraced gardens; imports guards for the spice. The harvest is escorted like a royal progress.
Exports glass and mirrors of famous clarity; imports soda ash, and buys silence about the workshop methods.
Exports beer, malt, and barrels; imports hops and cooperage timber. The brewers' calendar is the civic calendar.
Exports honey, wax, and mead; imports grain. Candle-wax for the great temples is its quiet diplomatic lever.
Exports tea from the mist terraces; imports porcelain and horses. The first flush is pre-sold three years out.
Exports worked iron — tools, not blades, by ancient policy; imports ore and coal. The no-blades rule is tested every war.
Exports dyes: a purple from the shore snails, a red from the hill beetles; imports plain cloth and returns it transformed.
Exports ships, hulls, and shipwrights' apprenticeships; imports mast-timber and iron. Its yards will build for both sides of a war.
Exports lace and fine needlework; imports thread and patterns. A single collar can carry a farm's yearly value.
Exports sulfur, saltpeter, and careful assurances; imports inspectors from nervous neighbors.
Exports porcelain from guarded kilns; imports clay it insists is inferior to its own, and buys it anyway.
Exports cattle on the hoof down the old drove roads; imports salt and winter feed. The drovers are a power in the land.
Exports furs, honey, and tar; imports cloth and coin. The trapping lines are inherited and mapped in memory only.
Exports slate and roofing tile; imports grain. Its roofs are counted a public boast and inspected like teeth.
Exports parade armor and hunting arms of famous ornament; imports the plain steel it works from realms that can't match the finish.
Exports saffron by the pinch and fortune; imports labor every autumn for the maddening harvest.
Exports coal from honest seams; imports timber for props and songs for the pits. The seams run under the border, which is a problem.
Exports incense and temple goods; imports silver and pilgrims. The pilgrim road pays better than the goods do.
Exports pearls from cold-water beds; imports divers' widows' pensions, as the grim joke runs, and little else.
Exports law — its courts arbitrate foreign disputes for handsome fees; imports precedents, scandals, and clients.
Exports nothing gladly and imports less; a realm of stubborn self-sufficiency, poorer and prouder for it.
